coredata.dc:title: Red fluorescent carbon dots for tetracycline antibiotics and pH discrimination from aggregation-induced emission mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Lin
Shi, Lihong
Jia, Jing
Eltayeb, Omer
Lu, Wenjing
Tang, Youhong
Dong, Chuan
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: Red fluorescent carbon dots (R-CDs) have been developed for the “turn-on” determination of pH and tetracycline antibiotics (TCs), including tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), and chlortetracycline (CTC). The CDs were fabricated via one-pot hydrothermal treatment using neutral red and thiourea as precursors. As-obtained CDs possess excellent photobleaching resistance, good photostability and biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity. Importantly, the CDs displayed a remarkable emission enhancement upon the introduction of TCs based on aggregation induced emission enhancement (AIEE) mechanism. For TC, OTC, and CTC, the linear correlation detection ranges are 3 − 35 μM, 2 − 50 μM, and 5 − 50 μM, respectively, and the limit of detections are 12, 23, and 25 nM, respectively. The proposed method has been successfully applied to the determination of TCs in milk products. Furthermore, the CDs exhibited a distinct H+-sensitive feature, and have been used to construct fluorescent pH sensor in linear range of 6.0–8.0 with a pKa of 7.08. Notably, the nanosensor has been also extended to monitor TCs and pH in cellular systems, demonstrating the proposed methodology presents promising biosensing application prospect.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Turn-on
Tetracycline antibiotics
pH
Aggregation induced emission

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21874087 and 21575084 ), and Scientific and Technologial Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi (No. 2020L0006 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the multifunctional sensor for turn-on detection of intracellular TCs level and pH based on red fluorescent R-CDs.
(A) TEM image and HRTEM (top inset) of the as-synthesized R-CDs and the particle size distribution for R-CDs (bottom inset); (B) AFM image of the as-synthesized R-CDs (inset: height of R-CDs).
(A) FTIR spectrum of R-CDs; (B) XPS full-survey and high-resolution XPS of (C) C1s, (D) O1s, (E) N1s and (F) S2p for R-CDs.

(A) Excitation − emission spectra of R-CDs; (B) UV–vis absorption, PL excitation, and emission spectra of R-CDs. The inset in top site was the pictures of R-CDs under the sunlight and UV light, respectively.

(A-C) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs upon adding different concentrations of TC (from bottom to top, 0 − 35 μM), OTC (0 − 50 μM), and CTC (0 − 50 μM). Insert: the linear relationship between ΔF and the concentrations of (A) TC, (B) OTC, and (C) CTC. ΔF ((ΔF = F – F0, where F0 and F represent the FL intensity of the R-CDs in the absence and presence of TCs).
TEM images of R-CDs-TC(A), HRTEM images of R-CDs-TC(B), and AFM image of R-CDs-TC(C).

(A) Fluorescence spectral changes of R-CDs with the pH value reducing from 12.0 to 3.0 (λex = 538 nm); (B) Sigmoidal fitting of the fluorescence intensity at 538 nm; (C) UV–vis spectral changes of R-CDs with the pH value reducing from 12.0 to 3.0; (D) Relationship between the absorbance intensity of R-CDs with the pH value. Inset: linearity of response. (E) PL repeated cycles in the range of 5.0 and 9.0. (F) The PL intensity of R-CDs under pH 7.0 in the presence of metal ions, anions, and amino acids.
Confocal fluorescence images of 0.2 mg mL−1 R-CDs in SMMC7721 cells at pH (A) 5.0, (B) 7.0 and (C) 9.0.
(A) Confocal fluorescence images of 0.2 mg mL−1 R-CDs in SMMC7721 cells with (B) TC (35 μM), (C) OTC (50 μM) and (D) CTC (50 μM).
Comparison of the developed fluorescence method for the determination TCs with some previously published methods.
Recoveries Test of Three TCs in the Milk Samples with the Standard Addition Method Based on the R-CD Probes (n = 6).

head.ce:title: Red fluorescent carbon dots for tetracycline antibiotics and pH discrimination from aggregation-induced emission mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, and Center of Environmental Science and Engineering Research, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, and Center of Environmental Science and Engineering Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, and Center of Environmental Science and Engineering Research, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for NanoScale Science and Technology, College of Science and Engineering, Flinders University, South Australia, 5042, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for NanoScale Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for NanoScale Science and Technology, College of Science and Engineering, Flinders University, South Australia 5042, Australia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red fluorescent carbon dots (R-CDs) have been developed for the “turn-on” determination of pH and tetracycline antibiotics (TCs), including tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), and chlortetracycline (CTC). The CDs were fabricated via one-pot hydrothermal treatment using neutral red and thiourea as precursors. As-obtained CDs possess excellent photobleaching resistance, good photostability and biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity. Importantly, the CDs displayed a remarkable emission enhancement upon the introduction of TCs based on aggregation induced emission enhancement (AIEE) mechanism. For TC, OTC, and CTC, the linear correlation detection ranges are 3 − 35 μM, 2 − 50 μM, and 5 − 50 μM, respectively, and the limit of detections are 12, 23, and 25 nM, respectively. The proposed method has been successfully applied to the determination of TCs in milk products. Furthermore, the CDs exhibited a distinct H+-sensitive feature, and have been used to construct fluorescent pH sensor in linear range of 6.0–8.0 with a pKa of 7.08. Notably, the nanosensor has been also extended to monitor TCs and pH in cellular systems, demonstrating the proposed methodology presents promising biosensing application prospect.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines (TCs), well known as a broad-spectrum antibiotic, were first synthesized in the 1940s and have been generally used for treatment of infections caused by various pathogenic bacteria [1,2]. Owing to their advantages of broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and low-cost, TCs are widely used, especially in human medicines, feed additives, as well as sterilization [3,4]. Nevertheless, only a small amount of TCs is decomposed in the organisms, and the TCs residues will remain in surface water, animal foods and soil [5,6]. A growing number of data show that long term use of TCs from daily food including water, milk, eggs and vegetables can cause harmful side-effects, such as anaphylactic reaction, bacterial resistance, gastrointestinal disturbance, and yellowing of teeth, etc [7,8]. Owing to these side effects, multiple approaches such as mass spectrometry (MS) [9], Raman spectroscopy (RS) [10], and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [11] have been established to detect TCs. However, most of these assay technologies need professional skill, sophisticated equipment and time-consuming sample pre-treatment procedures. In contrast, fluorescence-based methods with a higher sensitivity and specificity have aroused wide attention. For example, Jia et al. [12] designed a dye-doped fluorescent nano-probe for detection of TC by using metal-organic framework (UiO-66) as a fluorescent internal standard, and the externally grafted lanthanide Eu3+ complex as a response signal. Mailk et al. [13] developed a conjugated polyelectrolyte via the palladium-catalyzed Suzuki cross-coupling polymerization method for detection of TC. The fluorescence quenching by TC was ascribed to efficient electron transfer from conjugated polyelectrolyte to TC via electrostatic/hydrogen bonding interactions. Xu et al. [14] prepared fluorescent Au/Pt nanoclusters capped with polyethyleneimine to monitor CTC by incorporating with Al3+. Unfortunately, these systems are subjected to complex synthesis, heavy metal toxicity, and relatively low luminescence quantum yield, etc. Thus, it is still a significant challenge to find new fluorescence probes for TCs detection.

As an important metabolic and cellular parameter, hydrogen ions concentration plays a vital role in cell migration, cellular growth and other cellular processes [15,16]. In living cells, various physiological processes work to keep pH value in a specific constant. Slight changes in intracellular pH may disturb the intracellular normal activity [17]. Thus, it is great significance to measure pH distribution and variation at the cell in better understanding the biological effects of pH gradients. For this reason, a variety of methods for intracellular pH sensing have been developed [18,19]. Among those approaches, fluorescent probes show unique advantages due to their high sensitivity, rapid response, and low cost [20,21]. In particular, organic fluorophores [22,23] and fluorescent proteins [24] have been extensively used to monitor pH values in living cells. However, in practical intracellular pH sensing, organic fluorophores suffer from some disadvantages, including the cytotoxicity of the probe itself, relatively narrow absorption spectra, and high susceptibility to photobleaching [25,26]. Fluorescent proteins have drawbacks of the complicated extraction and purification [27,28]. Consequently, it is imperative to develop a high-efficiency and simple fluorescent material for pH determination.

Carbon dots (CDs), a special group of fluorescent nanomaterials, have shown great application potentials in many fields due to their excellent performance characteristics, such as bright fluorescence, [29,30] high photo-stability [31,32], good biocompatibility [33], and excellent water solubility [34]. In particular, CDs find tremendous importance in sensing research because engineering surface functional groups endows them with special molecular recognition ability. At present, most of the CDs-based sensors have been constructed for single-mode sensing, such as ions [35,36], amino acids [37,38] and antibiotic [39,40] etc. In order to further enrich their detection function, much effort has been made for constructing multi-mode sensing platforms by a single CDs-based sensor. Pan et al. [41] designed a single CDs-based sensor which can be selectively quenched by Hg2+ in a photo-induced electron transfer manner, by lemon yellow in an inner filter effect manner, and by Fe2+ or H2O2 in an oxidative quenching manner. Li et al. [42] employed one CD-based sensor to construct a multi-mode sensing platform for the discrimination of rutin, quercetin, methotrexate, and temperature through different quenching mechanisms. Very recently, Meng et al. [43] developed a multi-mode CDs-based sensing platform for monitoring of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol, Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid via the inner filter effect. However, the above-mentioned multi-functional probes were based on quenching mechanism. Compared with turn-off sensors, turn-on sensors exhibit improved sensing efficiency because it is quite trouble-free to detect an evolving bright signal in dark background than to look for the weakening of already existing bright signal [44–46]. Therefore, it is fundamentally significant to generate CDs-based multi-functional nanoprobes with the “turn-on” detection mode.

Aggregation-induced emission (AIE), as a unique fluorescence system, has become increasingly attractive since it was conceptually termed by the Tangʼ group in 2001 [47]. Tang and coworkers discovered that a variety of luminogens emissive in the aggregated state, but they were nonluminescent in the dissolved state. The reason for this phenomenon is that the intramolecular rotation is banned and blocks the non-radiative path and meanwhile activates radiative decay in the molecular aggregate state [48]. Numerous organic molecules have been designed and exhibited AIE. However, few reports were involved in AIE of CDs. By using H2O2 to oxidize C60 in a mixture solution of NaOH and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide, Gao et al. [49] synthesized CDs and first discovered that CDs displayed AIE properties in the presence of adenosine triphosphate (ATP). Aggregation of the CDs was induced through electrostatic interactions upon addition of ATP, which reduced solvation of the hydrophilic functionalities of the CDs, decreased non-radiative decay, and thus increased PL emission. This research opens a new avenue for the development of CDs sensing systems. Subsequently, Zhang et al. [50] also proposed the similar AIE effect for boron-doped CDs with glucose. Due to the specific interaction between glucose and boronic acid groups on the CDs surfaces, CDs became severely aggregated and created rigid structure, thus restricting the intramolecular rotation and boosting the fluorescence greatly. From then on, some CDs based sensors have been constructed toward fluorescence turn-on response to various analytes based on AIE principle [51–53]. These pioneers have active impacts on AIE effect of CDs. Unfortunately, most of these CDs usually emitted short wavelength light, which seriously limited their applications in biology. Several groups reported red CDs, but the traditional method for preparing red CDs is complicated, included multistep, and time-consuming reactions, as well as inconvenient postsynthetic treatments [54,55]. Thus, developing a simple and convenient way to prepare CDs with long wavelength AIE effect remains highly desirable.

With this aim, we designed a novel “turn-on” fluorescent sensor for the selective detection of TCs and pH based on aggregation-induced emission (Scheme 1 ). The R-CDs were synthesized through a one-pot hydrothermal method using neutral red and thiourea as raw materials and exhibited red fluorescence, low cytotoxicity and extraordinary photostability. Amazingly, TCs were able to induce the aggregation of R-CDs and cause enhancement in the fluorescence of R-CDs. As far as we known, this is the first report of R-CDs for turn-on detection of TCs. Besides, as-prepared R-CDs displayed a sterling sensing ability to pH response. Furthermore, the obtained R-CDs have also been applied for the detection of intracellular TCs level and pH.

Neutral red and thiourea were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC), and other antibiotics were obtained from Macklin (Shanghai, China). The various antibiotic solutions were freshly prepared just before experiments. All the other reagents were of analytical grade.

The R-CDs were synthesized using hydrothermal method. Briefly, neutral red (0.1 g) and thiourea (0.1 g) were mixed with 20 mL of ultrapure water within a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) to complete the reaction at 180°C for 3 h. Next, the crude product was centrifuged at 8500 rpm for 20 min to remove impurity substance. Then clear supernatant extract was purified with ultrapure water through a dialysis bags (1000 Da) for 24 h. Finally, the R-CDs solution was stored at 4°C for further use.

The fluorescence analysis was conducted as follow: R-CDs was dissolved with PBS solution (10 mM, pH 7.0) and controled an ultimate concentration of R-CDs was 0.2 mg mL−1. TCs with different concentrations were added in the above prepared solution, fluorescence measurement of the R-CDs was progressed after 2 min and recored under excitation at 538 nm. The anti-interference ability of the sensor toward TCs was examined with a series of relevant antibiotics to replace TCs.

Solutions with various pH were prepared with 10 mM PBS. The obtained R-CDs solution was dispersed in the above mentioned pHs solution with a volume ratio of 1:9. After 2 min of incubation, the FL spectrum were collected under excitation at 538 nm. Measurements were conducted in triplicate.

The particle size and morphology of the R-CDs were observed by transmission electron microscopic (TEM) and atomic force microscope (AFM). Fig. 1 A shows that the R-CDs dispersed uniformly in a spherical shape without apparent aggregation and distributed in narrow distributions with an average diameter of 2.39 ± 0.23 nm. The lattice spacing of 0.22 nm revealed by the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (inset in Fig. 1A) is assigned to the (100) face of graphitic carbon [56,57]. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 1B and Fig. S1, the topographic heights (3.92–8.05 nm) of R-CDs in AFM image confirms that these prepared R-CDs contain many graphene layers [39]. XRD pattern showed a sharp peak at 2θ values 26° that corresponded to (002) planes of graphite structure, suggesting the disordered pattern of carbon dots which due to the addition of N- and O-containing groups (Fig. S2) [58–60].

Besides, the structural composition and elemental distribution of R-CDs were investigated through FT-IR spectroscopy and XPS. The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 A) showed the absorption peaks at around 618, 1106, 1403, 1746, 2849, 2918, and 3146 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibrations of CS, CO, CN, CO, SH, CH and NH, respectively [61–63]. The full survey XPS spectra (Fig. 2B) of R-CDs displayed strong signals of C1s (281.4 eV) and O1s (529.3 eV) as well as weak signals of S2p (165.6 eV) and N1s (398.5 eV), which suggested that the prepared R-CDs is mainly composed of C and O elements with a small amount of N and S elements. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) showed four-unit moieties: CC/CC (284.96 eV), CN (286.10 eV), CO (286.44 eV) and CO (286.90 eV). The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) is deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.77 eV and 532.78 eV, which belong to COH/COC and CO, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2E) could be separated into two peaks at 399.79 and 401.89 eV corresponding to CN and NH bonds, respectively. There are four peaks in the S2p spectrum (Fig. 2F) at 163.77, 164.91, 168.19 and 169.23 eV, owing to S2p3/2, S2p1/2, -C-SO3-C- and -C-SO4-C- species, respectively. Based on the results from structural analysis, we concluded that the as-prepared R-CDs consist of π-conjugated domains in their carbon cores and R-CDs surface might covered with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups as well as the sulfur and nitrogen containing groups. The small quantity of S element contained in the R-CDs may originate from thiourea. These results obtained were consistent with FT-IR analysis.

The photoluminescence (PL) properties such as UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of R-CDs have been investigated. The 3D emission profiles of R-CDs (Fig. 3 A) were recorded under different excitation in aqueous solution. It can be clearly observed that the PL emission remains unshifted regardless of the excitation, which might be ascribed to their uniform surface states and narrow size distribution. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of R-CDs displayed two absorption peaks centered at 221 nm and 270 nm, which were ascertained to the π-π* transitions of the CC in the core of the R-CDs. In addition, a broad absorption band covering the region from 400 and 600 nm can be observed. The maximal absorption peak of this band is at 522 nm, which is attributed to the n–π* transitions of the surface states containing CO, CO/CN and CS structures [64,65]. Meanwhile, the defect surface states trap the excited state energy and result in strong fluorescence. The R-CDs manifested optimal emission (λem) at 612 nm upon excitation (λex) at 538 nm. Therefore, 538 nm is set as the reasonable excitation wavelength in the whole experiment to realize a high sensitivity. The R-CDs solution exhibited orange red under sunlight, while strong red PL was observed under UV light (inset in Fig. 3B). Meanwhile, the prepared R-CDs has high quantum yield 22.5% with reference to Rhodamine B (Fig. S3). As well, the average lifetime of R-CDs was calculated as 1.46 ns, determined by the time-correlated single-photon counting assay (Fig. S4). This short fluorescence lifetime of the R-CDs indicates that the radiative recombination of excitons produces fluorescence.

The photostability of the R-CDs was investigated to evaluate their feasibility in practical application. As shown in Fig. S5, following UV irradiation (365 nm) for 60 min, the solution showed no significant loss of fluorescence intensity. Besides, in Fig. S6, the FL intensity of R-CDs remains stable in a set of NaCl solution ranging from 0 to 3.0 mM, which indicated that the R-CDs can be stable even in a high sodium ion environment.

The analytical applicability of R-CDs has been examined in the search for the possible application as sensors. Surprisingly, as a label-free fluorescent probe, R-CDs can directly detect TCs (including TC, OTC and CTC). As seen in Fig. 4 (A–C), when the TCs concentration increased, the emission intensity at 610 nm was linearly enhanced with range of 3 − 35 μM for TC, 2 − 50 μM for OTC and 5 − 50 μM for CTC, and LODs of TC, OTC, and CTC as to be 12, 23, and 25 nM, respectively (S/N = 3). The results demonstrated that the R-CDs can be employed as a“turn-on” sensor for sensing TCs. As seen in Table 1 , it can conclude that the prepared R-CDs are comparable to many of the published reports with turn-off detection mode. To evaluate the selectivity of the R-CDs toward TCs, the other antibiotics were tested under the identical conditions. From Fig. S7, we can observe that only TCs caused a remarkable fluorescence enhancement of R-CDs, whereas other antibiotics showed negligible or no influence. This result indicated that the R-CDs had high selectivity for TCs.

To investigate the reason behind the highly selective fluorescence enhancement of R-CDs by TCs, TEM images of the R-CDs with TCs were studied. As seen in Fig. 5 A and Fig. S8, compared with the size of R-CDs, the particle sizes of R-CDs-TCs increased from 2.86 nm to 9.42 nm, 9.56 nm and 8.56 nm, respectively, implying that the R-CDs aggregated and assembled in an orderly fashion into large nanoparticles upon the addition of TCs. Besides, the HRTEM images of R-CDs-TCs (Fig. 5B and Fig. S9) exhibited many lattices with the same spacing but in different directions which indicated that the aggregation of R-CDs did not affect the spacing between adjacent lattice planes of R-CDs. Furthermore, a similar aggregation phenomenon was also found in AFM (Fig. 5C and Fig. S10). As shown in Fig. S11, the hydrated particle size increased about ten times after adding TCs. These results strongly confirm the aggregation of R-CDs under addition of TCs. As revealed Fig. S12, the zeta potential of R-CDs aqueous solution increased dramatically from −4.22 to +3.33, +3.61 and +3.70 mV with the introduction of TC, OTC and CTC, respectively, probably owing to the consumption of the carboxyl or other negative groups on the surface of R-CDs in the presence of TCs. Given in the molecular structure of TCs in Fig. S13, TCs have large π-conjugated structures. Through electrostatic interaction, TCs have a strong π-π stack interaction with R-CDs to induce the aggregation of R-CDs. In addition, we studied the change in the absorbance spectrum (Fig. S14). It was found that the absorbance at ca. 240−400 nm of R-CDs are increased after adding TCs, indicating the formation of bigger size, which can absorb and scatter the incident light source [66]. Meanwhile, as observed in Fig. S15 and Table S1, there was no significant change in fluorescence emission decay curves and lifetime data bofore and after addition of TCs. The results suggested that the interaction between R-CDs and TCs does not change the electron recombination pathway of R-CDs. The fluorescence emission comes from the same emission center, but it has higher emission efficiency. All the results support the “turn on” detection for TCs is attributed to AIE mechanism, that is, the increase of fluorescence emission is due to aggregation state suppresse the non-radiative decay. The aggregation of R-CDs can restrict the rotation of the functional group on the surface of R-CDs and enhance the π-conjugation, which lead to increase the radiative transition rate and decrease the nonradiative transition rate [67]. These results strongly evidenced that TCs induced the aggregation of the R-CDs and enhance the fluorescence of R-CDs.

With different pH values of 3.0–12.0, the fluorescence spectrum of the R-CDs was measured at excitation of 538 nm. Interestingly, the R-CDs showed a strongly pH-dependent PL behavior. As demonstrated in Fig. 6 A and B, the PL intensity of the R-CDs change slightly in the pH range of 3.0–5.0, dramatically decreases as the pH increases from 5.0 to 9.0, and finally tends to be gentle in strong base. The pKa value was determined as 7.08 with a linear correlation coefficient of 0.998. The colour of solutions at pH 5.0 and pH 9.0 changes from pink to yellow, which can be distinguished by the naked eye (inset in Fig. 6B). This phenomenon is conducive to the realization of dual-mode pH sensing.

The sensing property of R-CDs under different pHs was also evaluated by the UV–vis absorption spectra. Fig. 6C shows the absorption spectrum of the R-CDs at pH 3.0–12.0, which observed the increase of pH value induces a significant decrement at peak around 520 nm, meanwhile resulting in a marked rise at peak around 436 nm. As depicted in Fig. 6D, the absorption intensity ratios of the two absorption peaks (A520 nm/A436 nm) gradually decreased with the increase of the pH value which displayed a good linear relationship Y = −1.29X + 10.38 in the range of pH 5.0–7.4. As revealed in Fig. S16, the CDs solution shows a color transition from pink to yellow in daylight with pH variation. These results proved that the R-CDs can be used to detect pH with the “naked-eye”. In addition, the comparison details of the proposed sensor with other previously reported pH sensors are summarized in Table S2. The emission wavelength of our R-CDs is longer than those of CDs, making them preferable for sensing and bioimaging applications. More importantly, most of the reported pH sensors based on CDs worked in the acidic conditions which cannot be applied for monitoring cytosolic pH changes in live cells.

Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 6E, the pH-sensitive behavior of the R-CDs exhibits satisfactory reversibility. The pH values of R-CDs solution were adjusted by adding acid or base, and the fluorescence switching operation can be repeated from pH 5.0 to pH 9.0. As for intracellular sensing applications, the anti-interference capability was studied. As displayed in Fig. 6F, the PL intensities of the R-CDs had no significant changes upon the addition of common interferences, such as metal cations, anions, and amino acids. The results indicate that R-CDs have good chemical stability and good specificity for hydrogen ions concentration.

We further studied the mechanism of pH responsive property from the perspective of the aggregation-disaggregation. TEM images were taken and the zeta potential of R-CDs under different pH values were measured. As shown in Fig. S17, the R-CDs are well separated at pH 9.0. While the pH value is decreased to 5.0, individual R-CDs self-assemble into larger particles. The aggregation of R-CDs induced by pH was obvious. Besides, the surface states of the R-CDs were confirmed by the zeta potential values. The large number of carboxylic and hydroxyl groups on the surface of R-CDs can result in a negatively charged surface. As shown in Fig. S18, the zeta potential of the R-CDs at pH 3.0 is +2.76 mV, and with the increase of pH the zeta potential showed a negative trend. The prepared R-CDs, bring negatively charged, repel one another and oppose aggregation by virtue of their electrostatic interactions in alkaline conditions. But they are attracted to hydrogen ions by their opposite charges, and then they stack together compactly in acidic conditions. These findings are in accordance with TEM images and support the aggregation induced luminescence mechanism. Hydrogen ion induced the aggregation of the R-CDs and resulted in a stronger fluorescence at a lower pH.

To verify the analytical performance of fluorescence turn on CDs nanosensors, milk was selected using the standard addition method. A known amount of TCs were spiked into the milk samples, which were pretreated before detection. The results are shown in Table 2 . The recoveries of various concentrations of TCs in milk samples are in the range of 93.4–107.6%, with RSDs less than 6% (n = 6). The excellent results imply that the proposed method is reliable and applicable for the detection of TCs in real samples.

Cytotoxicity assessment is an important aspect on showing the feasibility of R-CDs in biomedical applications. We assessed the cytotoxicity of the R-CDs using HeLa cells as the model by the standard MTT assay. The cytotoxicity data reveals that as the R-CDs concentration increased to 400 μg/mL, the cell viability percentage kept above 80% (Fig. S19). The data proved the low cytotoxicity of R-CDs and could be applied in cell imaging and biosensing.

The cell imaging pictures were captured by a laser confocal microscope. As shown in Fig. 7 , it is clear observed that the intracellular fluorescence was gradually quenched after treating HeLa cells with the R-CDs for 30 min under various pHs (5.0, 7.0 and 9.0). Besides, in accordance with Fig. 8 , with incubation of the cells with TC, OTC, and CTC respectively, it was discovered the intensity of the FL signal in cells were enhanced, which proved that the fluorescence change in vivo was consistent with those in vitro. These good results implied that the R-CDs can be used to detect pH and TCs in living cells.

In summary, a novel “turn-on” fluorescent sensor based on R-CDs was developed for the selective detection of TCs and pH. The R-CDs with red emission were prepared via hydrothermal treatment using neutral red and thiourea as carbon source. In the presence of TCs, the fluorescence of R-CDs can be significantly enhanced based on the AIEE principle. Besides, the R-CDs showed a distinct pH-sensitive luminescence emission feature in the pH range of 6.0–8.0 with a pKa of 7.08. The good photostability and high biocompatible endued R-CDs successfully achieve bioimaging in living cells. Moreover, the R-CDs showed potential as an ideal candidate for building up multi-sensing platform for the detection of TCs and pH-related diseases.

Lin Li: Conceptualization, Data curation, Project administration, Writing - review & editing, Writing - original draft. Lihong Shi: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Jing Jia: Investigation, Validation, Data curation. Omer Eltayeb: Software. Wenjing Lu: Supervision. Youhong Tang: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Chuan Dong: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Shaomin Shuang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874087and21575084), and Scientific and Technologial Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi (No. 2020L0006).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.129513.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of highly efficient fluorescent carbon dots for tetracycline detection
Environ Sci Pollut Res.

J. Mater. Chem. C.

J. Phys. Chem. C.

Sens. Actuators B Chem.

ACS Omega

Talanta

Dye. Pigment.

Anal. Methods

Talanta

sb:title.sb:maintitle: S, N-Doped carbon dots for tetracyclines sensing with a fluorometric spectral response
Microchem. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.J. Xing, L. Huang, S.J. Zhao, J.F. Xiao, M.H. Lan, S, N-Doped carbon dots for tetracyclines sensing with a fluorometric spectral response, Microchemical Journal. 157 (2020) 105065–105073.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lin Li obtained B.Sc. degree in Chemistry from Changzhi University in 2015. Now she is a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in analytical chemistry.

Lihong Shi is now working as a professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. She obtained her Ph.D. degree in Physical Chemistry from Shanxi Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2008. Her current research interests include nanomaterial and chemosensors.
Jing Jia obtained B.Sc. degree in chemistry from Northcentral University in 2016. Now she is a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in analytical chemistry.
Omer Eltayeb is a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in analytical chemistry.

Wenjing Lu is now working as a lecturer in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. She obtained her PhD degree in analytical chemistry from Shanxi University in 2019. Her current research interests include nanomaterial.
Youhong Tang is now working as a professor in Flinders University, South Australia 5042, Australia. His current research interests include AIE nanomaterials.

Chuan Dong is a professor of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. He obtained his Ph.D. degree at Shanxi University in 2002. Dong has published over 200 papers and 100 patents and his current research interests are biosensors, chemosensors, optical fiber sensor, fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence, and environmental analysis and monitoring.

Shaomin Shuang is a professor of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. She received her Ph.D. degree in South China University of Technology in 1998. She has published extensively in journals such as Nanoscale, Journal of Materials Chemistry B, Chemical Communications, Sensors and Actuators B, Analytical Chemistry, Talanta etc. Her research interests are in the areas of analytical chemistry, supermolecular chemsitry, and photochemsity.
